bidirectional_dilemma.context: If I watch my diet, I will maintain good health. If I consume excessive junk food, I will become more susceptible to illnesses. We know that either I am committed to watching my diet or I am not prone to illnesses, but it is uncertain which of these statements is true. It is possible that I am only dedicated to maintaining a healthy diet, or alternatively, I may not be prone to illnesses, or even both statements could be true.
bidirectional_dilemma.question: Can we say at least one of the following must always be true? (a) i will stay healthy and (b) i don't eat too much junk food

It is uncertain which one of these conditions is true, as it could be the case that only one of them is true or both of them are true simultaneously.

The only thing that was certain was that either Harry would go to the park or he would go to the beach. It was unclear which option he would ultimately choose, as it could be that he would go to the park alone, go to the beach

However, the exact combination of whether only option one is true, or only option two, or if both options are true, remains unknown.

constructive_dilemma.context: Roman had a choice to make - whether to take a walk in the garden or a stroll in the city. If he decided to take a walk in the garden, he would be able to enjoy the fresh air. On the other hand, if he chose to take a stroll in the city, he would get to experience the hustle and bustle. It was uncertain which option Roman had gone with, but what was clear was that he had made a decision. Either Roman took a walk in the garden or he took a stroll in the city, or perhaps he even did both. Regardles
constructive_dilemma.question: Can we say at least one of the following must always be true? (a) he get to enjoy the fresh air and (b) he get to experience the hustle and bustle

hypothetical_syllogism.context: If Adam goes for a swim, he will become tired. Consequently, he will decide to take a nap to rest.
hypothetical_syllogism.question: If adam goes for a swim, does this entail that he will take a nap?

completions.text:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Adam's swimming leads to him taking a nap. The problem gives us two conditional statements linking swimming to tiredness and tiredness to napping.

The first conditional tells us that swimming is sufficient for tiredness. This will let us move from a swimming action to the tired state, which is needed for the second rule.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Adam swims, then he becomes tired.</text>
<logic>tired(adam) :- swimming(adam)</logic>
</step>

The second conditional states that tiredness guarantees a nap. Once we have tiredness, this rule will allow us to infer that Adam will nap.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The second rule says that if Adam is tired, then he will nap.</text>
<logic>napping(adam) :- tired(adam)</logic>
</step>

We are told explicitly that Adam does go for a swim. This fact activates the first conditional, giving us a concrete starting point.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>We are told that Adam goes for a swim.</text>
<logic>swimming(adam)</logic>
</step>

Applying the first conditional to the fact that Adam swims, we conclude that Adam must be tired. This is a direct use of modus ponens: the antecedent (swimming) is true, so the consequent (tired) follows.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From Adam swimming and the first rule we infer that Adam is tired.</text>
<logic>tired(adam)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Adam is tired, we can use the second conditional. Because tiredness leads to napping, we infer that Adam will nap, again by modus ponens.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since Adam is tired, the second rule lets us conclude that Adam will nap.</text>
<logic>napping(adam)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Adam is napping, so the conclusion is yes.
Answer: Yes
</think>

Answer: Yes

completions.text:
<think>
We are given two conditional statements about Adam's possible activities. Our goal is to determine whether the first conditional leads to the second one in this particular scenario.

The first rule tells us that swimming guarantees tiredness. This is essential because it connects the activity we will later assume (swim) to the property that will trigger the second rule.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Rule 1 states that if Adam swims, then he becomes tired.</text>
<logic>tired(adam) :- swims(adam)</logic>
</step>

The second rule establishes that tiredness leads to the decision to nap. Knowing this allows us to see how the first rule's consequence feeds into the conclusion we need to reach.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Rule 2 states that if Adam is tired, then he decides to take a nap.</text>
<logic>naps(adam) :- tired(adam)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Adam swims is the concrete observation we have. It serves as the starting point for applying the conditional rules that follow.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Adam swims.</text>
<logic>swims(adam)</logic>
</step>

By combining the fact that Adam swims with the first rule, we can apply modus ponens to infer that Adam is tired. This step bridges the initial observation to the intermediate property needed for the second rule.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From Adam swimming and the first rule, we infer that Adam is tired.</text>
<logic>tired(adam)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Adam is tired, we can use the second rule. Again, modus ponens lets us conclude that Adam will take a nap, directly linking his tired state to the desired outcome.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Using Adam's tiredness and the second rule, we infer that Adam will take a nap.</text>
<logic>naps(adam)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Adam is naps, so the conclusion is yes.
Answer: Yes
</think>

Using Adam's tiredness and the second rule, we infer that Adam will take a nap.

material_implication.context: Once upon a time, there was a guy named John who was very conscious about his weight. He knew that if he didn't eat healthy, he wouldn't be able to maintain his desired weight. John understood the condition that if he didn't eat healthy, he wouldn't be able to gain weight.
material_implication.question: Based on context, can we say, at least one of the following must always be true? (a) he is eating healthy and (b) he will not gain weight

completions.text:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether John satisfies either of the two statements: that he is eating healthy or that he will not gain weight. To answer this, we must use the two conditional facts provided in the context and see how they interact.

The first premise tells us that if John fails to eat healthy, then he cannot maintain his weight. This conditional connects the property of not eating healthy directly to the impossibility of keeping his weight, which will be useful later when we consider his actual weight status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if John is not eating healthy, then he cannot maintain his weight.</text>
<logic>~maintains_weight(john) :- not_eating_healthy(john)</logic>
</step>

The second premise states that a lack of healthy eating also implies that John cannot gain weight. This gives us another link between the unhealthy‑eating condition and the weight‑gain situation, which will allow us to combine with the first premise.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if John is not eating healthy, then he cannot gain weight.</text>
<logic>~gains_weight(john) :- not_eating_healthy(john)</logic>
</step>

The context explicitly says that John is maintaining his weight. This concrete fact will serve as the starting point for applying the first conditional, enabling us to work backward and infer something about his eating habits.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>John is currently maintaining his weight.</text>
<logic>maintains_weight(john)</logic>
</step>

Since we know John is maintaining his weight, and the first rule tells us that not eating healthy would make it impossible to maintain weight, we can use modus tollens: if maintaining weight were impossible under the condition, then the condition itself must be false. Therefore we conclude that John must be eating healthy.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From the fact that John maintains his weight and the rule that not eating healthy would make it impossible to maintain weight, we can infer that John must be eating healthy.</text>
<logic>eating_healthy(john)</logic>
</step>

Having established that John is eating healthy, we can now consider the second conditional. The rule says that not eating healthy would prevent weight gain, but because John is eating healthy, the condition for that rule does not hold. Hence, we can infer that the negation of weight gain is also satisfied, meaning John will not gain weight.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since we now know John is eating healthy, the rule linking not eating healthy to not gaining weight tells us that he will not gain weight.</text>
<logic>~gains_weight(john)</logic>
</step>

We derived Eating_healthy(john)
We derived Not_gains_weight(john)
Thus John is eating healthy or John will not gain weight is derived
Answer: Yes
</think>

Mason actually receives his salary.
Since Mason receives his salary, but the rule says leaving would mean no salary, we can infer that Mason did not leave his job.
Therefore, we conclude that Mason did not leave his job.
